reasoning: The base rate of a vaccine being approved and added to Wikipedia within 1 to 3 months is extremely low unless it's already in late-stage Phase 3 trials and expecting data/approval imminently. My searches confirmed that there are no such prophylactic Mono vaccines on the precipice of approval. Moderna's mRNA-1189 only entered Phase 3 trials sometime in mid-2024 to mid-2025. A Phase 3 trial for a vaccine against an infectious disease like EBV (measuring the prevention of Mono) will require at least 1-2 years of follow-up on thousands of participants, followed by months of regulatory review. WGc-043 was approved as an mRNA therapeutic for EBV-related cancers (advanced solid tumors) and not as a prophylactic vaccine against Mono, meaning it would not change the "Epstein-Barr virus infectious mononucleosis (Mono)" entry on the "List of infectious diseases" Wikipedia page. There is effectively zero chance of a vaccine being developed and listed on Wikipedia by April 2026. I assign a very low probability (2-2.1%) to account for edge cases like extreme regulatory acceleration, emergency use for some unimaginable reason, or Wikipedia vandalism/editor error.
